summary: DHH argues that social media has destroyed the ability to feel unique within small social circles, creating a global competition for distinctiveness that has unhealthy consequences. In pre-internet times, minor quirks or interests were enough to make someone 'something' within their small group. Now, constant exposure to the entire world's talent and uniqueness dilutes individual distinctiveness. He suggests this drives people toward adopting diagnoses and oppression narratives as identity markers, and calls for a return to smaller, isolated circles where ordinary variations can feel meaningful.
key_points: Before the internet, uniqueness only required standing out within a small social circle — liking sausage or comic books was enough
Social media collapsed all local hierarchies into one global competition for distinctiveness, making ordinary uniqueness feel worthless
The drive for uniqueness may be fueling the trend of adopting mental health diagnoses and oppression identities as personal branding
The Instagram critique of unrealistic achievement highlight reels may have been preferable to the current search for uniqueness through affliction
We need to find ways to restore isolated circles of belonging where being a slight variation on ordinary is enough
money_quotes: This scene pins the deep fear in most people that the worst you can possibly be is ordinary.
When your comparison group was mainly a small group of friends, or maybe your class, or at most your school. It wasn't the whole damn world.
The best of everything, the most unique species of every subcategory, bubble to the top. Thus diluting whatever would have made you special in your little group in an instant.
One way to stand out as unique is to embrace and accentuate every quirk of personality, every sliver of peculiarity with a diagnosis that can form an integral part of one's personal branding.

Where the social ideal used to be that above all you wanted to be 'normal' – but with a dash of quirkiness to just stand out a little! – it now often appears that in some of the social games being played it is about being as 'weird' as possible.
In a global competition for uniqueness, there's a constant game of one-upmanship running to find the next facet to highlight why we're actually special.
Maybe searching for uniqueness in the stratosphere of accomplishment was still superior to searching for it in an identity of oppression or diagnosis.
If we can't find a way to make being mostly ordinary okay, we're condemned to search for that 'something' in ever more pathological places.
key_insight: Social media's collapse of local social hierarchies into one global arena has made ordinary uniqueness impossible, driving people toward increasingly pathological ways of standing out.
